FPPC O~INIONS 195 
BEFORE THE FAlH POLrrnCAL PRACTICES 
Cm'1:'lISSIO~-J 
In the Hatter 
) 
) 
Opinion requested by 
) 
Robert L. Zenz, Deputy General Manager, ) 
California State Employees Association 
) 
O t= • 
-'- . 
No. 75-156 
December 3, 1975 
BY THE CO.HHISSIO:-:J: 
hTe have been asked the follm·:ing 
question by Robert L. Zenz, Deputy General Manager, California 
State Employees Association: 
The California State Employees Association has appointed 
one member in each of the state's 40 senatorial districts to 
serve as employee legislative advocates. 
These advocates will 
not be paid, but reDnbursement for limited expenses will be 
provided and reported by the Association. 
Employee advocates 
will be selected by the Association's chief lobbyist and will 
work under his direction. 
Is an employee legislative advocate prohibited from 
making or arranging contributions (Government Code Section 
86202) or gifts (Government Code Section 86203) to public 
officials because he is supervised by ~ registered lobbyist? 
CO~CLUSION 
The agent of a lobbyist may not make or arrange a con-
tribution or gift which the lobbyist would be prohibited from 
making under Government Code Sections 86202 or 86203 unless 
it is clear that the contribution or gift is not intended to 
further the goals of the lobbyist and thus is outside the scope 
of the agency relationship. 
AN1,LYSIS 
It appears from the facts as stated that the employee 
advocates are not themselves lobbyist.s within the meaning of 


No. 75-156 
Page Two 
1 F 
C OPINIONS 19? 
Government Code Section 820391:/ because one of the essential 
ele~ents of the definition of a lobbyist is that he must 
receive economic consideration. 
See opinion requested by 
Georae G. Hardie, Golden State Grevhound Association, 1 FPPC 
Opinrons 140 (No. 75-003, Oct. 23,-19~5). Obviously, if 
the employee ad~ocates were lobbyists, they would be subject 
to all of the obligations and prohibitions of Sections 86100, 
et seq. 
In the instant case, the employee representatives re-
ceive instructions, training and lobbyist materials directly 
from the association's chief lobbyist. 
They receive instruc-
tions from no other association official and any reports or 
questions they may haVe are sent by the representative directly 
to the chief lobbyist. 
Under these circumstances, the employee 
representatives are agents of the chief lobbyist. 
As defined by California Civil C'ode Section 2295: 
"An 
agent is one who represents another ,called the principal, in 
dealings with third persons." 
See, e.g., Store of Happiness v. 
CarmOllu. S. Allen, 152 Cal.App.2d 266-:--269 (1957). 
Transu.ctions 
performed by an agent within the scope of the agency relation-
ship are attributable to his princip~l. 
Gipson v. Davis Realty 
Co., 215 Cal.App.2d 190, 205-06 (1963). 
Accordingly, restrictions 
imposed on the lobbyist bind all 'dho act as his agents under his . 
direction. 
To conclude otherwise would permit the lobbyist to 
accomplish, indirectly through his agents, that which he is 
prohibited from accomplishing directly. 
Therefore, the agent 
of a lobbyist is prohibited from making any contributions (Sec-
tions 86200, 86202) and is restricted from making gifts aggre-
gating more than $10 in a calendar month (Sections 86201, 86203). 
Moreover, since gifts of an agent are attributed to his principal, 
all of the gifts by the principal and his agents, when aggregated, 
may not exceed the $10 limit. 
If, while pursuing the goals of 
his principal, an ag~nt makes a gift worth $10 to a legislator, 
the principal and all of his agents will be prohibited from 
making any other gifts to that legislator for the remainder of 
the month in which the gift was made. 
However, an agency relationship does not inextricably 
bind two persons for all purposes. 
Agency relationships are 
usually limited in scope; for example, the agency relationship 
in this case is limited to influencing legislators with regard 
to the California State Employees Association's legislative 
goals. 
Thus, restrictions on the principal apply to his agent 
only to the degree that the agent is pursuing the principal's 
goals. 
All statutory references are to the Government Code 
unless otherwise noted. 


No. 75-156 
Page 'l'h2::'ee 
• 
1 
C OPINIONS 197 
t'le observe, however, that although a lobbyist_'s agent 
may therefore independently make a gift or contribution to a 
legislator, such actioI1S, in many cases, undoubtedly would 
be connected with the agent's official duties. 
The agent is 
actiEC) independen tly of the lobbyist only ,-;hen it is clear 
from the surrounding circumstances that he is not seeking to 
advance the goals of the lobbyist 02::' acting or. the instrLlctions 
of the lobbyist. 
Approved by the Commission on December 3, 1975. 
Concurring: 
Brosnahan, Carpenter, Lowenstein and Miller. 
Commissioner ~'laters was absent. c ~ n 
/'~: tV1/'JJ~ 
Daniel H. Lmvens tein 
Chairman

sections.conclusion: The agent of a lobbyist may not make or arrange a contribution or gift which the lobbyist would be prohibited from making under Government Code Sections 86202 or 86203 unless it is clear that the contribution or gift is not intended to further the goals of the lobbyist and thus is outside the scope of the agency relationship.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether employee legislative advocates working under the direction of a registered lobbyist are prohibited from making contributions or gifts to public officials.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Agents of a lobbyist are bound by the same contribution and gift restrictions as the lobbyist principal, but only to the extent they are pursuing the principal's goals; independent gifts or contributions are permitted if they are clearly not intended to advance the lobbyist's objectives.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR document with clear question and conclusion sections. Opinion No. 75-156 dated December 3, 1975. Commission approved with four concurring commissioners; one absent.

CONCLUSION: The agent of a lobbyist may not make or arrange a contribution or gift which the lobbyist would be prohibited from making under Government Code Sections 86202 or 86203 unless it is clear that the contribution or gift is not intended to further the goals of the lobbyist and thus is outside the scope of the agency relationship.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that employee legislative advocates supervised by a registered lobbyist are agents of that lobbyist and thus prohibited from making contributions and restricted on gifts in the same manner as the lobbyist, unless their actions are clearly independent of the lobbyist's goals.
